title: Algebraic Structures Associated to Weinstein Manifolds
speaker: Yasha Eliashberg

proof_stages.label: Weinstein and Stein setup
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines a Weinstein structure as a symplectic manifold with a complete Liouville vector field gradient-like for an exhausting Morse function, explains how Stein manifolds provide such structures, and notes the possibility of exotic Weinstein structures.

proof_stages.label: Reduction to Legendrian handle data
proof_stages.summary: Weinstein Morse theory gives a handle decomposition with isotropic or Lagrangian cores; subcritical pieces are topologically controlled, so the essential symplectic information is concentrated in the Legendrian attaching spheres or links of the critical handles.

proof_stages.label: Symplectic homology from Reeb orbits
proof_stages.summary: Symplectic homology is recalled in a Bourgeois-Oancea style model whose generators are doubled Reeb orbits on the contact boundary and whose differential counts holomorphic cylinders, anchored curves, marked-point configurations, and Morse-theoretic caps.

proof_stages.label: Legendrian DGA and vector-field complex
proof_stages.summary: For a one-critical-handle attachment, the Legendrian contact homology DGA generated by Reeb chords is reinterpreted as functions on a noncommutative graded space, and the cyclic module of vector fields with the induced Lie-derivative differential is asserted to compute the symplectic homology of the resulting Weinstein manifold.

proof_stages.label: Poly-tensor enhancement and convolution bracket
proof_stages.summary: The construction is extended from vector fields to differential forms and arbitrary noncommutative tensor fields, producing dual/cohomological complexes and a convolution Lie bracket, with an “excited” vertex introduced to break cyclic symmetry and control signs.

proof_stages.label: SFT Hamiltonian and Maurer-Cartan deformation
proof_stages.summary: Counts of holomorphic disks and more general disk configurations with Reeb-chord punctures and auxiliary gradient trajectories define an element H in the Lie algebra satisfying a Maurer-Cartan equation, so the old differential can be deformed to d+[H,-].

proof_stages.label: Product from selected Hamiltonian components
proof_stages.summary: The product on symplectic homology is recovered by applying the specific components H_{1,2} and H_{2,0} through the same excited-vertex convolution/gluing operations to two inputs.

examples.name: Stein submanifold of affine space
examples.note: A Stein manifold embedded in complex affine space with the restricted squared-norm plurisubharmonic potential is used to make concrete how Stein geometry produces a Weinstein structure.
examples.engagement_reason: he constructs the Weinstein structure on a Stein affine submanifold by restricting the ambient quadratic potential/Kähler form and taking its gradient Liouville field

examples.name: Exotic Weinstein structures on Euclidean space
examples.note: Known constructions of Weinstein structures on smooth R^{2n} not homotopic or symplectomorphic to the standard C^n structure illustrate that Weinstein geometry can contain information beyond smooth topology.
examples.engagement_reason: he cites known exotic Weinstein structures on R^{2n} as nonhomotopic or nonsymplectomorphic to the standard one without analyzing a specific structure

passes.reason: These are systematic nonstandard Weinstein structures on R^{2n} used to disprove uniqueness of the standard structure.

Nonstandard Weinstein structures on topological Euclidean space are used to disprove uniqueness or standardness, but they are not sporadic classification exceptions.

Exotic Weinstein structures on R^{2n} are used to refute uniqueness/standardness of Weinstein structures on Euclidean space, but they form systematic exotic examples rather than classification exceptions.

examples.name: Changing the Morse presentation of C^n
examples.note: In response to an audience question, the speaker contrasts the one-minimum presentation of C^n with one containing a cancelling pair of critical points of indices n-1 and n to clarify that the subcritical piece depends on the chosen handle presentation.
examples.engagement_reason: he changes the Morse presentation of C^n by adding critical points of indices n-1 and n and identifies the resulting subcritical part up to homotopy

passes.reason: Standard C^n is the symmetric 0-handle base case, and the cancelling-pair presentation refutes invariance of the subcritical piece.
The changed presentation is still trivial C^n with a canceling handle pair and rebuts invariance of the subcritical piece.
The altered Morse presentation of standard C^n uses cancelling handles on a trivial base case to show the subcritical piece is not presentation-independent.
The altered Morse presentation of C^n shows the subcritical piece can depend on the chosen presentation rather than on the final manifold.
Changing the Morse presentation of C^n uses the simplest Euclidean Weinstein manifold with canceling handles to show that the subcritical piece need not be presentation-invariant or perfect.
The underlying C^n is the contractible one-0-handle base case, used to show that the subcritical part can change with the Morse presentation.
Changing the Morse presentation of standard C^n rebuts invariance of the subcritical piece, while C^n with one minimum is the minimal, highly regular Weinstein base case.
used to show a subcritical piece depends on the Morse presentation, with C^n serving as the trivial Weinstein base case
standard C^n is the maximally symmetric minimal Weinstein model, and the altered Morse presentation answers the claim that the presentation must be perfect or unique
the standard contractible C^n with cancelling Morse handles is used against the idea that the subcritical presentation is fixed or perfect
It rebuts presentation-invariance by using the standard minimal Weinstein model C^n with an added canceling handle pair.
The C^n presentation with only a minimum versus an added canceling pair shows the subcritical part is not presentation-invariant, and the one-handlebody model is a minimal base case.

examples.name: Stabilization of a Stein manifold by C
examples.note: The assertion that multiplying a Stein manifold by C destroys the unstable exotic behavior and yields a topologically controlled Weinstein/Stein situation illustrates the subcritical h-principle from a complex-analytic viewpoint.
examples.engagement_reason: he states the stabilization fact that multiplying a Stein manifold by C makes the Weinstein structure standard/topological without deriving it

passes.reason: The C-stabilized product is subcritical/topological and shows exotic Weinstein features need not survive stabilization.
Stabilization by C makes the Weinstein structure subcritical and shows exotic symplectic information need not persist stably.
Stabilizing by a trivial C factor makes the Stein/Weinstein manifold subcritical and is used to show exotic phenomena can disappear under stabilization.
Stabilization by C is subcritical and kills symplectic distinctions, showing exotic Weinstein features need not survive stabilization.
Stabilizing by C is a systematic subcriticalizing operation that kills the nontrivial Weinstein features and is presented as an instability phenomenon rather than a direct counterexample.
Stabilizing by C makes the Weinstein structure subcritical/topological, showing that exotic symplectic distinctions can disappear under stabilization.
Stabilizing by C is used to show exotic Weinstein behavior can disappear under product, and the resulting stabilization is subcritical and h-principle-like.
used to show exotic symplectic features can disappear after product with C, and such stabilizations are subcritical/topological
stabilizing by C makes the Weinstein manifold subcritical, showing exotic symplectic information can disappear under stabilization
stabilizing by C is used as a positive illustration that the resulting Weinstein manifold becomes subcritical/topological
It shows exotic Weinstein features can disappear after C-stabilization, which is subcritical and symplectically trivialized.
Stabilizing by C is used to show exotic Weinstein features can disappear, and the product becomes subcritical/flexible with collapsed symplectic topology.

examples.name: Flexible C^0-small perturbation of a Legendrian attaching link
examples.note: A small formally Legendrian-isotopic perturbation with a spanning Lagrangian disk is described to illustrate how tiny changes in the attaching Legendrian can turn the resulting Weinstein manifold flexible and erase symplectic invariants.
examples.engagement_reason: he describes perturbing a Legendrian attaching link C^0-small, keeping it formally Legendrian isotopic, spanning it by a Lagrangian disk, and obtaining a flexible Weinstein manifold

passes.reason: The loose flexible perturbation kills rigid symplectic data despite being C^0-small and formally unchanged.

The C^0-small loose/flexible perturbation has h-principle-type trivial symplectic behavior and is used to show failure of robustness under tiny Legendrian changes.
The C^0-small Legendrian perturbation shows formal or tiny changes need not preserve symplectic topology, and the resulting flexible Weinstein object has reduced rigid structure.

examples.name: R^{2n} versus cotangent bundles for symplectic homology
examples.note: The contrast between trivial unfiltered symplectic homology for full Euclidean space and the cotangent-bundle case recovering free-loop-space homology illustrates why symplectic homology is interesting even without the action filtration.
examples.engagement_reason: he only contrasts the known symplectic homology outcomes for R^{2n} and cotangent bundles, saying one is trivial and the other relates to free loop space homology

passes.reason: Standard R^{2n} is the symmetric trivial baseline, while cotangent bundles refute the expectation that unfiltered symplectic homology is always trivial.
Standard R^{2n} is the trivial highly regular Weinstein model contrasted with cotangent bundles to refute triviality of unfiltered symplectic homology.
The contrast between trivial SH for highly regular R^{2n} and nontrivial SH for cotangent bundles is used against the expectation that unfiltered symplectic homology is uninteresting.
Standard R^{2n} is the flat subcritical zero-SH benchmark, while cotangent bundles give nonzero SH and refute general triviality of unfiltered symplectic homology.

The contrast with R^{2n}, the maximally symmetric trivial symplectic-homology base case, is used alongside cotangent bundles to refute the idea that unfiltered symplectic homology is always uninteresting.

Cotangent bundles are contrasted with R^{2n} to refute the expectation that unfiltered symplectic homology is always trivial, with R^{2n} serving as the minimal symmetric zero case.
used to refute the expectation from R^{2n} that unfiltered symplectic homology is always trivial, with R^{2n} the trivial symmetric model
